personality_voice.book_baseline: Yaxley in the books is blunt, brutish, and status-hungry. He is direct and assertive in Voldemort's presence, eager to demonstrate his value ('seemed determined to receive some portion of approval'). He is casually cruel and contemptuous toward those he considers inferior - his treatment of Cattermole in the lift is cold, threatening, and laced with blood-purity bigotry delivered without heat or drama, as if it is simply the natural order. He is not theatrical like Bellatrix; his menace is matter-of-fact. He draws his wand quickly at perceived threats (the peacock) and snorts dismissively at Lucius's extravagance. His speech is blunt and functional: short declarative sentences, commands, and conditional threats.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of this personality reasonably well. In the Atrium scene he is menacing, direct, and contemptuous toward Cattermole/Ron, delivering his threat about the wife's blood status with cold authority. His line 'You do realize I'm on my way downstairs, don't you, Cattermole?' mirrors the book's tone. In the Malfoy Manor scene he is a background presence. In the forest clearing he silences Hagrid with a wand flick and a curt 'Quiet!' - consistent with his blunt, functional cruelty.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses the specific texture of Yaxley's status-hunger and his eagerness to impress Voldemort. The book's Malfoy Manor scene gives him multiple lines where he pushes his intelligence about Dawlish, squares his shoulders, and visibly seeks approval - a behavioral pattern that defines him. The film collapses this to a single interjection ('I have heard differently, my lord') with no follow-up about the Imperius Curse on Thicknesse, no planted Ministry agents, and no visible striving for Voldemort's approval. The film also omits the peacock moment and the snort about Lucius, which add texture to his personality. The Atrium scene is somewhat compressed but tonally faithful. The blood-purity contempt line ('Spare us. The brats of Mudbloods do not stir our sympathies') is absent from the film corpus.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The dominant traits - blunt cruelty, directness, contempt for inferiors - are preserved in the Atrium and forest scenes. However, the status-hunger and approval-seeking pattern, which is a secondary but observable trait in the books, is largely absent from the film due to the compression of the Malfoy Manor scene. This is a minor but real simplification. Score in the 18-22 range.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for core traits; reasonably inferred for the degree of simplification in the Manor scene.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Yaxley serves two structural functions: (1) an intelligence-providing Death Eater who competes with Snape for Voldemort's favor at the Manor meeting, and (2) a direct physical threat and plot catalyst - his accidental entry into the Fidelius Charm at Grimmauld Place forces the trio to abandon their safe house, a major plot consequence. He also participates in the Muggle-born persecution apparatus at the Ministry and fights in the Battle of Hogwarts. He initiates actions (leaning forward to challenge Snape's intelligence, placing the Imperius Curse on Thicknesse, sealing the Ministry exits, pursuing the trio into the Floo network, patrolling the forest).
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves his role as a minor antagonist and physical threat. He appears at the Manor meeting with one line of intelligence. He threatens Cattermole at the Ministry. He seals the exits and pursues the trio into the Floo network - and crucially, his grabbing Hermione's robe is shown, which is the cause of the Grimmauld Place compromise (confirmed in the film's visual action). He reports to Voldemort in the forest and silences Hagrid.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The most significant agency loss is the removal of his Imperius Curse on Thicknesse. In the books, this is Yaxley's major proactive contribution to Voldemort's plan - it is his intelligence coup, the thing that earns him Dolohov's congratulations and partial approval from Voldemort. In the film, Thicknesse simply appears as already under control, with no attribution to Yaxley. This is a transferred/omitted action. His role in the Ministry persecution (the courtroom scene where he jeers and participates as a prosecutor alongside Umbridge) is also absent from the film corpus, reducing his function in that subplot. His Grimmauld Place consequence is preserved visually.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The Imperius Curse on Thicknesse is a meaningful act of agency that is omitted - it is Yaxley's one proactive plot contribution beyond physical threat. The courtroom role is also absent. These are real reductions. However, his physical threat function and the Grimmauld Place consequence are preserved. Score in the 10-13 range.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; directly evidenced for the omission of the Thicknesse plot contribution.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Yaxley's motivations are straightforward: loyalty to Voldemort, desire for status and approval within the Death Eater hierarchy, and genuine blood-purity ideology. He is not conflicted - he believes in what he does. His eagerness to impress Voldemort ('seemed determined to receive some portion of approval') and his casual cruelty toward Muggle-borns are consistent expressions of these motivations. There is no internal conflict; he is a true believer and a careerist.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film does not contradict these motivations. His behavior in the Atrium (threatening Cattermole over blood status) and at the Manor (offering intelligence) are consistent with the same motivational profile. The forest scene shows continued loyalty.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not develop or explore his motivations beyond what is shown behaviorally. The status-hunger motivation is less visible due to the compression of the Manor scene. However, the core motivations are not changed or contradicted.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified by omission rather than altered. The blood-purity ideology and Voldemort-loyalty are still legible from the film material. Minor simplification but not a change. Score in the 11-13 range.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from behavioral evidence in both book and film.

character_arc.book_baseline: Yaxley has a flat arc. He begins as a loyal, status-seeking Death Eater and ends as one. He gains no insight, undergoes no change, and his trajectory is one of continued service to Voldemort until defeat. He is defeated at the Battle of Hogwarts (slammed to the floor by George and Lee Jordan). This flatness is intentional - he is a functional antagonist, not a character with a developmental arc.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film also presents a flat arc. He appears, serves Voldemort, pursues the trio, reports in the forest, and is presumably defeated off-screen. No arc is invented or imposed.
character_arc.difference: The arc is flat in both, which is correct. The film does not show his defeat at the Battle of Hogwarts explicitly (the book has him slammed to the floor by George and Lee Jordan), but this is a minor omission that does not alter the arc's shape.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Flat arc preserved as flat. Minor omission of the defeat moment, but this does not change the arc. Score in the 11-13 range (compressed but faithful, with the defeat beat missing).

key_relationships.book_baseline: Yaxley's key relationships are: (1) Voldemort - subordinate seeking approval, competitive with other Death Eaters for favor; (2) Snape - implicit rivalry at the Manor meeting, where Yaxley challenges Snape's intelligence and is publicly corrected; (3) Dolohov - paired with him in the forest, receives congratulations from him at the Manor; (4) the trio - as a physical antagonist, particularly the Grimmauld Place consequence with Hermione.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Voldemort relationship is present but the approval-seeking dynamic is reduced to one line. The Snape rivalry is present in the film - Yaxley offers competing intelligence and Snape corrects him - but it is compressed. The Dolohov pairing in the forest is preserved. The antagonist relationship with the trio is preserved through the Ministry and Floo chase.
key_relationships.difference: The Snape-Yaxley rivalry is the most meaningful relationship in the books and it is present but compressed in the film. The approval-seeking dynamic with Voldemort is reduced. The Dolohov relationship loses the congratulatory moment (which came from the Thicknesse revelation). These are simplifications rather than fundamental changes in dynamic.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics are recognizable but compressed. The Snape rivalry is present. The Voldemort subordination is present. The Dolohov pairing is present. No relationship is fundamentally altered in emotional tone, but the Thicknesse omission removes the moment that most clearly defines his standing among peers. Score in the 7-8 range.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for the degree of compression.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Yaxley is not a complex character, but the books give him specific texture: the status-hunger and approval-seeking at the Manor; the casual, bureaucratic cruelty in the lift (threatening a man's wife as leverage for an office repair); the contemptuous snort at Lucius's peacocks; the quick wand-draw at a noise; the jeering laugh in the courtroom; the resentful look when Voldemort turns back to Snape; the Imperius Curse achievement. These details build a portrait of a mid-level authoritarian - competent, cruel, and hungry for recognition - that is more specific than a generic Death Eater.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the bureaucratic cruelty in the Atrium scene reasonably well. The forest scenes are functional. The Manor scene is reduced to one line. The courtroom scene is absent. The Thicknesse achievement is absent. The resentful look and the approval-seeking pattern are absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses the courtroom scene (jeering laugh, 'Spare us' line), the Thicknesse achievement, the resentful look at Voldemort, the peacock snort, and the multi-beat approval-seeking at the Manor. What remains is the Atrium cruelty and the physical threat function. The character is recognizable but reduced to fewer defining moments.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The lost material - particularly the courtroom participation and the Thicknesse achievement - removes the two scenes that most clearly show Yaxley as a specific type of authoritarian rather than a generic Death Eater. The approval-seeking texture is largely gone. This is a noticeable simplification. Score in the 8-10 range.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for absences; directly evidenced for what is preserved.

lost_or_transferred_material: Yaxley's placement of the Imperius Curse on Pius Thicknesse - his major proactive intelligence contribution at the Manor meeting - is omitted entirely from the film

Yaxley's multi-beat approval-seeking at the Malfoy Manor meeting (squaring shoulders, leaning forward, pushing back against Snape's dismissal, receiving Dolohov's congratulations) is reduced to a single line
Yaxley's role as a prosecutor/interrogator in the Muggle-born courtroom scene alongside Umbridge is absent from the film
The 'Spare us. The brats of Mudbloods do not stir our sympathies' line from the courtroom is absent
Yaxley's resentful look when Voldemort turns back to Snape after dismissing his intelligence is absent
The peacock snort and the quick wand-draw at a noise - small texture moments establishing his personality - are absent
Yaxley being slammed to the floor by George and Lee Jordan at the Battle of Hogwarts is absent from the film corpus (though this may occur off-screen)
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function or act of agency is transferred/omitted): applied - the Imperius Curse on Thicknesse is Yaxley's single most significant proactive contribution and is omitted entirely
comparative.key_observations: Yaxley's film portrayal is recognizable and tonally consistent in the scenes that are included - the Atrium cruelty and the physical threat function are well-preserved. However, the film loses the two scenes that most distinguish him from a generic Death Eater: his role as a prosecutor in the Muggle-born trials and his achievement of placing Thicknesse under the Imperius Curse. The latter is his only major proactive plot contribution and its omission is the most significant faithfulness gap. The Malfoy Manor scene is compressed to a single line, removing the approval-seeking behavioral pattern that gives him specific texture. What remains is a functional but simplified version of the character - cruel, blunt, and physically threatening, but stripped of the bureaucratic ambition and institutional cruelty that make him specific in the books.
